# Interview — Dale Hutchinson

---

**Good morning, Dale. Thanks for making the time — I know you're not exactly sitting around waiting for meetings, so I'll make sure this is worth your while.**

**The quick version of what we're doing: we're working through the finance team systematically to document how the function actually operates — the real processes, the way they genuinely work day to day, not the org chart version. The goal is to make sure the institutional knowledge that lives in people's heads gets properly captured, so it's visible, protected, and can be built on. It's not a performance review, it's not a headcount exercise — it's genuinely about making sure the expertise that exists in this function doesn't walk out the door if someone's hit by a bus or takes a week off.**

**You've been embedded in underground production for fourteen years, which puts you closer to the actual cost drivers than almost anyone else in finance. That's exactly the kind of knowledge we need to get down properly.**

**Before we get into any specifics — can you just give me a picture of your role in your own words? What you're responsible for, and roughly how your time is structured across a typical week? Take your time with it.**

*leans back in chair, nods slowly*

Right. Yeah, that framing actually makes sense. And fair point — you're right, I'm not sitting around.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

So my role is basically: I sit between the production team and the central finance machinery and make sure the numbers they're generating make sense, and that they understand what they're spending. 

Look, the production department — they're focused on getting ore out of the ground safely and on schedule. They're not thinking about GL codes or cost centre structures. But at the end of every week, they need to know whether they're tracking to their budget, where the blowouts are, what's coming. That's mine. And at the end of every month, I need to make sure every dollar that's hit the books for their operations is accurate before it gets locked.

*settles forward*

How my time actually breaks down: Monday morning is the big one. I pull the prior week's actuals from Pronto — labour, materials, contractors, equipment, all of it — and I reconcile it against what I was expecting. I've built a scorecard model that does a lot of that heavy lifting, and then I send a summary out to the Superintendent and the Mine Manager by 10. They use that to see if they're on track. That's probably two to three hours every Monday.

The rest of the week is... variable. I'll spend time with the shift bosses or the Superintendent if something doesn't look right, or if they're building a case for new equipment or contractor work. Then I'll help them model the cost impact, write it up.

Month-end is different — probably four or five days of solid work. I'm in Pronto, checking postings, raising accruals for contractor costs we know are coming, back and forth with Sarah in central finance to get adjustments booked. I finish my variance report by the third working day after month-end, and that's usually a half-day effort — pulling it together, writing it up.

*shrugs*

And once a year, October into November, I'm basically locked in budgeting with the Superintendent. Six weeks of building forecasts — how many metres are we developing, how many tonnes, what's the equipment plan — and translating that into cost centre numbers.

The rest is... noise. Meetings, requests for numbers, explanations when someone's confused about a variance.

Does that cover what you're after?

**That's a really useful picture, Dale — thank you. You've clearly got a well-structured rhythm to it.**

**Before we go deep on any one of those, I want to make sure I've got the full inventory mapped out first — that way we don't accidentally spend all our time on Monday mornings and run out of time for something equally important.**

**So you've mentioned: the weekly actuals pull and scorecard on Mondays, the cost queries and business case modelling that comes up through the week, month-end — accruals, checking postings, the variance report — and then the annual budget build in October-November. That's a solid shape of it.**

**A few quick questions just to fill out the inventory before we go deeper:**

**On top of those — are there any quarterly processes, or anything tied to forecast updates mid-year? Some sites run reforecasts at Q2 or Q3 — does that land on your desk at all?**

*scratches chin, thinks for a moment*

Not formally, no. We don't do a structured reforecast cycle — at least not one that comes through me as a standalone process. 

*leans back*

What happens in practice is a bit messier. If something material changes operationally — like we hit a fault zone that changes the development plan, or a piece of equipment goes down longer than expected — the Superintendent and I will talk through it, and I might rebuild sections of the budget model to show what that means for the rest of the year. But that's ad hoc, not scheduled.

Central finance sends down a reforecast request sometimes — I want to say maybe mid-year, maybe not every year — asking for updated numbers. When that lands, the Budget Analyst is usually the one coordinating it. I'll get asked to provide updated production activity forecasts — development metres, that sort of thing — and I feed those to the Analyst. But I'm not the one running the full rebuild. That happens more at the central level.

*pauses*

Which, honestly, is where some of the disconnect happens. I've got pretty good visibility into what's actually going to happen underground for the rest of the year, but by the time central's got a reforecast, it's already been filtered through a few layers. So sometimes the updated budget doesn't quite match reality by the time it gets back to the Superintendent.

But to your question — no, there's no quarterly cycle that's really mine. It's mostly the Monday rhythm and the month-end stuff, with the big annual push in October.

Does that answer it?

**Yeah, that answers it — and that observation about the reforecast disconnect is useful, I've noted it. We'll come back to the mechanics of how that information flows when we get into the budget process.**

**A couple more inventory questions and then we'll get into the detail:**

**The business case modelling you mentioned — equipment, contractor work — how often does that actually come up? Is it a handful of times a year, or more frequent than that?**

*shifts in seat*

It varies. Some years it's quiet, some years it's not.

*thinks back*

Last year we probably did... four, maybe five proper business cases? New jumbo drill, extension of a contractor team for development work, a request to bring in additional mucking equipment. The year before was lighter — maybe two or three. This year so far we've had one, and there's probably another one brewing for later in the year.

*gestures vaguely*

It's not predictable because it depends on what breaks, what the Superintendent thinks they need to hit production targets, what the Mine Manager thinks the operation can support. Sometimes it clusters — like, if we're planning a major shift in how we're tackling a section of ore, that might trigger three or four cases in a month. Sometimes you'll go three months without anything.

*leans forward slightly*

What I will say is: when they do come, they usually come to me first because the Superintendent or the shift bosses know I can throw together a rough model pretty quickly. Does it make sense financially? What's the payback? How does it affect our monthly opex profile? I'll usually have something back to them within a day or two — Excel model and a one-pager.

Then depending on the dollar value, it either goes to the Mine Manager for sign-off, or if it's bigger, it goes to the Finance Manager as well. But that's not really my call — the Superintendent usually knows where it needs to go.

Does that give you a sense of the frequency?

**It does, yeah. And the escalation path there is useful — we'll come back to the mechanics of how those cases are built when we get to it.**

**Last couple of inventory questions:**

**Is there anything tied to contractor management specifically — I'm thinking things like reviewing contractor invoices against what was actually done underground, or signing off on contractor hours? Or does that live somewhere else?**

*shakes head*

Not really my job, no. That's more... it depends on the contractor type, actually.

*leans back, thinking*

For labour contractors — the ones doing development or stoping — the shift supervisors are tracking their hours on timesheets. Those timesheets come to me, but mainly because I use them to estimate the accrual at month-end. I need to know: how many hours did they work, what's the rate, what cost centre does it hit? But I'm not the one validating whether they actually worked those hours or checking the quality of the work. That's the shift boss's job.

*pauses*

For equipment contractors — hire companies, mobile drill teams, that sort of thing — the invoices come in through Accounts Payable, and they get coded to the right cost centre. I'll look at them at month-end if something looks odd, but again, I'm not the one signing off that the work was done. That's usually the Production Superintendent or whoever ordered the equipment.

*shifts slightly*

Where I do interact is if an invoice doesn't match what I was expecting cost-wise, or if a contractor's work is running over the estimated hours. Then I might flag it with the Superintendent and we'll talk through whether that's a scope creep issue or just how it's panning out. But that's more of a conversation than a formal sign-off process.

So — to answer your question directly — no, that's not really a core part of my role. I use contractor data to build accruals, but the actual contract management and hours verification, that's not mine.

**Got it — so you're a consumer of that data for accrual purposes, but the validation happens elsewhere. That's a useful distinction.**

**One more and then we'll get into the detail — is there anything more ad hoc or event-triggered that we haven't mentioned? Things like safety incident cost tracking, insurance claims, any kind of capital project cost monitoring? Or anything that lands on your desk that you'd describe as "that's just mine and nobody else really knows it exists"?**

*pauses, rubs jaw thoughtfully*

Hmm. Not really on the safety or insurance side — that's handled elsewhere, thank God. That's more Risk and Compliance territory.

*sits up a bit*

Capital projects... look, we don't really run major capital projects through underground production. That would be a site-level thing. If we're talking underground equipment that's capitalised, that goes through the capex approval process and then it's managed by the engineering team. I might provide cost information to support a business case, but the ongoing tracking isn't mine.

*pauses, then adds*

The thing that's probably closest to what you're asking — and this might fall under "nobody else really knows it exists" — is my cost centre remapping layer.

*shifts forward, a bit more guarded now*

The way Pronto's cost centre hierarchy is structured, it doesn't really match how the production team thinks about their work. They think in terms of stoping areas, development drives, equipment types. But the cost centre chart is... it's more administrative. So I've built a remapping in my scorecard that translates Pronto postings into the structure that actually means something to the Superintendent and the shift bosses.

*looks directly at interviewer*

That's something that lives in my spreadsheet, and it's not documented anywhere. If I disappeared, someone would have to reverse-engineer it.

Is that the kind of thing you're looking for?

**Yes — exactly that kind of thing. That's genuinely valuable to surface, and I appreciate you flagging it directly.**

**That remapping layer is something we'll definitely want to document properly — the logic behind it, what it maps from and to, how it feeds the scorecard. We'll come back to that when we go through the Monday process in detail.**

**So to summarise the inventory before we get into it:**

- **Monday weekly actuals and scorecard** — the core weekly rhythm
- **Month-end** — accruals, posting checks, variance report
- **Annual budget build** — October-November with the Superintendent
- **Ad hoc business case modelling** — a few times a year
- **The cost centre remapping layer** — underpinning all of the above, lives in the scorecard

**Does that feel like the complete picture, or is there anything sitting at the edges we haven't named yet?**

*nods slowly, then pauses*

That's... yeah, that's pretty much it. 

*thinks for a moment*

There's one thing that's more of a pain point than a formal process, but it's worth naming: central finance sometimes reallocates costs between my cost centres without telling me. Usually it's Sarah doing some kind of allocation or recharge, or the Budget Analyst moving something for reporting purposes. I don't find out until I'm deep into the month-end reconciliation and my numbers don't tie to what I was tracking.

*slight edge of frustration in voice*

That's not really a process I run, it's something that happens to me. But it affects everything downstream — throws off my variance analysis, makes me question whether the scorecard's right, creates noise I have to chase down.

*leans back*

And related to that — there's the accrual submission piece. At month-end I'm sending Sarah a spreadsheet with contractor accruals — GL account, cost centre, amount, description. I've asked twice if we can use a shared template for that so there's less back-and-forth, but it's never materialized. So every month I'm building the same spreadsheet format by hand and emailing it over. Small thing, but it adds friction.

*looks up*

Those aren't really processes I own, but they're in my world. Does that matter for what you're mapping?

**It absolutely matters — both of those are useful observations and I've noted them. The unannounced reallocation issue affects the integrity of your variance analysis, and the accrual submission format is a recurring manual step with no good reason to stay manual. Both worth capturing.**

**Right — let's get into the detail. I want to start with the Monday process because it sounds like it's the backbone of your weekly rhythm, and it's also where that remapping layer lives.**

**Take me back to Monday morning. What actually triggers you to start — is it a calendar alarm, is it a system notification, is it just habit at this point?**

*small smile*

Habit, mostly. I'm usually in by 7:15 or 7:30 on a Monday. The shift changes over at 6, so by the time I get here the underground crews have been going for a bit and I've got a clear picture of what the prior week actually was.

*settles back*

I'll make a coffee, sit down at my desk, and I'll log into Pronto. First thing I do is pull the cost posting report for the prior week — so last Monday I'd pull everything posted for the week of the 16th through the 22nd. I've got a standard query I run, filters it to my 15 cost centres, pulls labour, materials, contractors, equipment — everything.

*gestures at screen*

That dump comes out as a CSV or Excel export. Takes maybe ten minutes. Then I drop it into my master spreadsheet — and this is where the remapping happens — and the spreadsheet automatically categorizes each posting into the structure that makes sense operationally.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

The logic in there... it's a mix of formulas, some manual lookup tables, some conditional rules that I've refined over years. GL account codes map to operational categories, cost centres get regrouped, and then the system calculates actuals against the phased budget I built at the start of the year.

*leans forward*

By about 9 o'clock, I've got a clean picture. I can see where we are against budget by production area, by cost type, where the variances are sitting. Then I build the summary — usually a one-page version with a couple of charts — and I send that to the Superintendent and the Mine Manager by 10.

*sits back*

That's the rhythm. Every Monday, like clockwork.

**Good — that's a clear picture of the flow. Let me make sure I've got the detail right before we move on.**

**The Pronto query — is that a saved query you've built, or are you manually setting the filters each time? And when you say it covers your 15 cost centres, is that number fixed, or does it change if production areas change?**

*nods*

It's a saved query. I built it years ago and I just run it as-is every Monday. Saves time — I'd rather not have to remember the filter logic every single week.

*gestures vaguely toward the screen*

The query pulls all postings to a specific range of cost centre codes that I set up when I first built the system. So yeah, it's fixed. If the production structure changes — and it does, occasionally — I'd have to go back into the query and update the cost centre range.

*pauses, thinks*

That's actually happened a couple of times. Like, five or six years ago we reorganized how the underground was divided up operationally, and I had to rebuild parts of the cost centre list. Took me a day to get it right and make sure the historical data still tied.

*leans back*

The 15 cost centres — that's been pretty stable for a while now. It covers the main stoping areas, the development drives, a few support functions like maintenance and ventilation. But it's not like it's hardcoded. If the Superintendent decides to split a production area or merge two, that's a conversation I'd need to have about how to adjust the cost centre structure.

*looks directly at interviewer*

And that's where the remapping layer matters. Pronto's hierarchy doesn't change easily, but my scorecard can translate it into whatever operational grouping makes sense. So I can work with small changes without having to redo the whole query.

Make sense?

**Makes sense — so the saved query is the fixed extraction layer, and the remapping in the spreadsheet is where the flexibility lives. That's an important distinction.**

**Now, the spreadsheet itself — when you drop that CSV in, what actually happens? Are you pasting it into a specific tab, and then the formulas pick it up from there? I want to understand the mechanics of how that remapping actually works.**

*pauses, shifts in chair slightly — a bit more protective now*

Right. Yeah, so there's a data tab where the raw CSV goes. I paste it in, it's got all the GL postings with amounts, cost centre codes, descriptions, dates — everything from Pronto.

*gestures but doesn't point to screen*

Then there's a separate calculation tab where the magic happens. The formulas pull from the raw data tab, they match GL codes and cost centre codes against lookup tables I've built, and they aggregate everything into the operational structure — the stoping areas, the development groups, the equipment categories.

*leans back, slightly guarded*

It's... it's a mix of SUMIF, some index-match stuff, some conditional logic. I've layered it over time as I've learned more about how the data comes through and where the complications are.

*pauses*

The thing is — and I'm not trying to be difficult here — but the logic in there is pretty intricate. There are rules about how certain GL codes get treated differently depending on which cost centre they hit, there's some manual overrides for things that don't fit the pattern. It's not something I could just hand you a screenshot of and have it make sense.

*looks at interviewer directly*

Not because I'm trying to keep it secret, but because it genuinely took years to build and it's got a lot of context embedded in it. Is that something you're going to want me to walk through in detail, or are we mapping this at a higher level?

**That's a completely fair thing to flag, and I appreciate the honesty. For today, we're mapping at the process level — I'm not asking you to walk me through every formula. What we need to capture is enough that someone could understand what the spreadsheet does, what goes in, what comes out, and what the key logic layers are — not a line-by-line audit.**

**What you've described already is useful: raw data tab takes the Pronto CSV paste, calculation tab applies GL code and cost centre lookups against lookup tables you've built, aggregates into operational categories, and compares against the phased budget. That's the skeleton.**

**Two things I want to make sure I've got right on that:**

**First — the phased budget that you're comparing actuals against. Where does that live, and how does it get into the spreadsheet? Is that another tab you built at the start of the year, or is it pulled from somewhere?**

*nods*

It's another tab in the same scorecard spreadsheet. I build it during the annual budget process — October, November — and it lives in there.

*leans forward*

So the way it works is: during budget season, I work with the Superintendent to forecast activity — development metres per week, tonnes per week, equipment utilization, that sort of thing. We build those forecasts out by week for the whole year, cost them out, and that becomes the phased weekly budget.

*gestures*

It's not just a flat annual number divided by 52. It's actually phased — some weeks we're doing more development, some weeks we're in production stoping, maintenance schedules hit at different times. The budget reflects that.

*sits back*

So at the end of that budget process in November, I've got a tab in the scorecard that says "Week 1: development budget is $X, production budget is $Y, total is $Z." And then every Monday, when the actuals come in, the comparison formulas just pull from that budget tab and calculate the variance.

*pauses*

The budget tab itself — it gets rebuilt every year. The Superintendent and I go through it together and agree on the phased numbers. Then that's what I'm comparing against all year.

*looks up*

Now, the thing is: those phased budgets are built in a separate Excel model during the budget process itself. Completely different file. And then I manually translate the final numbers into the scorecard tab structure. So there's a translation step there that's... manual.

Does that make sense, or do you need me to clarify that part?

**That makes sense — and that manual translation step is worth noting. So there are actually two separate Excel files: the budget build model you work in during October-November with the Superintendent, and then the scorecard that runs all year. And the link between them is a manual transcription rather than a live connection. I've got that.**

**The phased budget tab — once it's in the scorecard at the start of the year, does it ever get updated mid-year? You mentioned earlier that if something material changes operationally — a fault zone, equipment down — you might rebuild sections. When that happens, are you updating the budget tab in the scorecard directly, or does that stay locked and you track the variance separately?**

*scratches jaw, thinking*

Good question. It stays locked, mostly. The budget tab is the baseline for the year — that's what we committed to back in November, and that's what we track against.

*shifts in seat*

If something material changes operationally and we're rebuilding the forecast, I'm doing that in a separate model, not touching the scorecard budget tab. So I might have a side model that says "okay, if we lost three weeks to that fault zone, here's what the revised forecast looks like for the rest of the year." But that's separate.

*pauses*

What gets reported back to the Superintendent and the Mine Manager is still the variance against the original budget. So if we're $50k over in month three because of the fault zone impact, that shows as a negative variance on the Monday scorecard — it's explained in a conversation, but the scorecard itself doesn't change.

*leans back*

The reason I do it that way is: once you start changing the budget mid-year, you lose your baseline. You can't easily see cumulative year-to-date performance if the goalposts keep moving. And from a reporting perspective, central finance wants to see variances against the approved budget anyway.

*slight pause*

Now, sometimes if the variance is really material — like we're going to be significantly over or under for the full year — that information feeds into a reforecast request if central asks for one. But the scorecard budget tab itself stays put.

Does that work for you?

**It does — and the logic behind it is clear. Locked baseline, variance explained in conversation, revised forecast lives in a separate model if needed. That's a deliberate design choice, not an oversight.**

**Let me just close out the Monday process before we move on. You've walked me through the pull, the paste, the scorecard doing its work — by about 9 you've got a clean picture. Then you said you build a summary, one page, a couple of charts, and it goes to the Superintendent and Mine Manager by 10.**

**A few things I want to pin down on that last step:**

**That one-page summary — is that built inside the scorecard spreadsheet and then exported, or are you pulling numbers across into a separate document? And when you send it — is that an email with an attachment, or does it go somewhere else?**

*leans back*

It's a separate document — usually a Word doc, sometimes a PDF if I'm sending it up the chain. I pull the key numbers out of the scorecard — actuals vs budget by major category, the significant variances, maybe a couple of charts showing trend or breakdown — and I write a brief commentary.

*gestures*

It's not complicated. Usually something like: "Week 22 actuals came in at $480k, budget was $510k, we're tracking $30k ahead. Development metres are on track, but contractor costs for the ore drive were higher than expected — here's why." That kind of thing.

*shifts forward slightly*

And yeah, it's an email. I send it to the Superintendent and the Mine Manager, sometimes cc the Finance Manager if there's something unusual. Usually just an attachment — the summary doc and maybe a quick email body with any context.

*pauses*

I've been doing it this way for... I don't know, at least since I've been in this role. The Superintendent expects it Monday by 10, and he uses it in his 10:30 huddle with the shift bosses to talk through how the week went cost-wise.

*looks directly at interviewer*

The Word doc thing — I know that probably sounds old-fashioned. Central finance keeps asking me to use templates or post things to a shared system. But honestly, a one-pager that I can format quickly and send is faster than wrestling with some centralized reporting tool. And it works.

**Understood — and it clearly serves its purpose if the Superintendent's using it in his 10:30 huddle. I've noted the format.**

**One thing I want to make sure I've captured: the charts you include — are those built inside the scorecard and then copy-pasted into the Word doc, or are they built separately each week?**

*nods*

They're in the scorecard. I've got a couple of standard charts — one showing actuals vs budget by cost category for the current week and year-to-date, one showing the variance trend over the last four or five weeks. They're built off the calculation tabs, so they update automatically when I refresh the data.

*gestures vaguely*

I just copy them out of the scorecard and paste them into the Word doc. Takes maybe 30 seconds per chart.

*pauses*

I've had those charts in the same place in the scorecard for years, so it's pretty quick — just copy, paste into the template, done. The Word doc itself has a standard format too. Heading, a bit of commentary, the charts, maybe a table of the key numbers if there's something specific to call out.

*sits back*

It's repetitive, which I know sounds inefficient, but it's also fast and reliable. I know exactly what I'm producing, it looks the same every week, the Superintendent knows what to expect. There's something to be said for that.

*slight shrug*

Could I automate it more? Probably. But for a one-page summary that takes me maybe 45 minutes total to build and send every week, the effort to set up some kind of automated reporting pipeline doesn't really justify itself.

**Fair enough — and 45 minutes for a reliable weekly output that directly feeds a management meeting is not unreasonable. I've got a complete picture of the Monday process now.**

**Let me just do a quick recap to make sure I've got it right, and then we'll move to month-end:**

**Monday morning, you're in by 7:15 or 7:30. You run a saved Pronto query covering your 15 cost centres — labour, materials, contractors, equipment — export it as a CSV, paste it into the raw data tab of your scorecard. The scorecard's calculation tab applies GL code and cost centre remapping logic against lookup tables you've built, aggregates into operational categories, and compares against the phased weekly budget that lives in a separate tab. By around 9 you've got a clean variance picture. You then build a one-page Word doc — commentary, two charts copy-pasted from the scorecard, key numbers — and email it to the Superintendent and Mine Manager by 10, occasionally copying the Finance Manager. Total time roughly two to three hours.**

**Does that capture it accurately, or is there anything I've missed or got wrong?**

*nods slowly, considering*

That's... yeah, that's accurate. You've got it right.

*pauses*

One small thing I'd add, just for completeness: before I even pull the Pronto query, I usually scan through the prior week's postings manually — just a quick look to see if there's anything obviously wrong or out of place. A labour posting that's way bigger than I'd expect, a material cost that doesn't make sense for what was scheduled. Just a sniff test.

*leans back*

I've done this long enough that I know what a "normal" week looks like within about 2%. So if something jumps out, I'll dig into it before I even build the scorecard. Makes sure I'm not just perpetuating a coding error or a system glitch.

*gestures*

That's maybe 10 or 15 minutes before the formal pull. Not a huge amount of time, but it's part of the rhythm.

*looks up*

Other than that, yeah — you've captured it. Two to three hours, Monday morning, scorecard built, summary out by 10. That's the backbone of the week.

**Good catch — that's worth having in the record. So there's actually a manual review step that comes before the formal query pull: a quick scan of prior week postings directly in Pronto, looking for anything obviously anomalous, based on your pattern recognition from 14 years of knowing what a normal week looks like. Then the formal pull happens. I'll add that in.**

**Right — let's move to month-end. You described it as four or five days of solid work. I want to understand how that actually unfolds — what day it starts, what triggers it, and how it sequences through.**

**Take me to the last day of the month, or wherever this actually begins for you. What's the first thing that kicks it off?**

*settles back, thinking through the sequence*

Month-end officially closes the books on the third working day after month-end. So if the month ends on a Friday, we close on Wednesday. If it ends mid-week, it's three working days after that.

*leans forward*

What triggers it for me is the actuals becoming available in Pronto. Usually that's the day after month-end closes operationally — so if we're closing November 30th, by December 1st all the labour postings, material invoices, everything that was booked in November is in the system. That's when I start.

*gestures*

First thing I do — and this is day one, usually the 1st of the next month — is I run a full month-end report for all my cost centres. Not just a weekly snapshot like the Monday process. I'm pulling everything posted to production for the entire month, and I'm going through it line by line.

*pauses*

I'm looking for miscodes — labour that got posted to the wrong cost centre, materials that hit the wrong GL account. I'm looking for accruals that should have been raised but weren't. I'm looking for anything that doesn't smell right.

*sits back*

That first day is probably six to eight hours of just reviewing. By end of day I've got a list of things that need adjusting — usually a dozen or so items, sometimes more.

Does that match what you're asking about?

# Interview — Sarah Okonkwo, Management Accountant, Central Finance

---

*[Settling in, relaxed tone, unhurried.]*

Thanks for making the time, Sarah — I know close periods are never exactly quiet, so I appreciate it.

Just to frame this up quickly before we start: what I'm here to do is document how the finance function actually works — the real, operational picture. The goal is to capture the institutional knowledge that people like you carry, make it visible, and make sure it's properly recorded. That's useful for continuity, for onboarding, for identifying where the team's capacity could be better deployed. It's not a performance review, it's not an audit, and it's genuinely not about headcount. There are no wrong answers here — the more accurate the picture, the more useful the output.

I'll be taking notes as we go, and at some points I'll probably reflect back what I've heard to check I've got it right.

---

Let's start somewhere easy. Before we get into any specific processes, I'd just like to get a feel for your role in your own words.

**Can you give me a quick picture of what you're responsible for, and roughly how your time is structured across a typical week — or maybe a typical month, since I imagine the rhythm of your work follows the month more than the week?**

Take as long as you need — this is just to help me understand the shape of your role before we go into the detail.

*[Leaning back slightly, thinking for a moment]*

Yeah, okay. So fundamentally, I'm responsible for the technical close — making sure the numbers are right and the reporting pack gets out on time. That's the core of it.

I'd say my month breaks into two distinct rhythms. The first three weeks are relatively normal — I'm doing the ongoing reconciliations, I'm the point person for the external auditors if we're in audit season, I'm supporting the business partners with any accounting questions, that sort of thing. It's busy but it's manageable.

And then from day 25 onwards — really from the last few days before month-end through to day five — it's the close cycle. That's the intense period. The rhythm shifts completely. **On the vault custodian:** That's a separate function — not part of plant processing, not part of my team. They maintain custody of the doré from the moment it leaves the refinery circuit until it ships. I don't know their exact title off the top of my head, but their primary job is physical security and accounting for the bullion inventory.

All in ounces. I use the "pours completed" line item for the revenue accrual.

**On the reconciliation between produced and poured:** Yes, there's always a difference, and no, it doesn't require reconciliation in the sense of investigating discrepancies. It's expected.

Here's why: the production and the pouring are on different cycles. Let's say we produce 500 ounces of doré in June. It comes out of the circuit as batches — maybe 50 ounces one day, 45 the next, and so on. We don't pour every single batch immediately; we accumulate them. So maybe we poured 480 ounces in June, and 20 ounces is still in the holding tank at month-end, waiting to be consolidated with July production for the July pour.

That 20-ounce difference is real inventory. It's on the vault custodian's balance sheet as closing inventory and it becomes opening inventory for July. So at month-end:

- Met team says: produced 500 ounces in June
- Vault custodian says: received 500 ounces in June, poured 480 ounces in June, closing inventory 20 ounces
- I accrue revenue for 480 ounces in June

The 20 ounces in closing inventory will be poured in July and recognised as July revenue.

I do a sanity check on this — I take the vault custodian's opening inventory plus their received amount, minus their closing inventory, and that should equal pours. If it doesn't, I ask them why. But that's just arithmetic verification. I don't reconcile the Met team's production figure to the vault custodian's received figure because those are two different measurements taken at different points and they don't have to match in real time. The Met team measures what comes out of the circuit; the vault custodian measures what they physically receive. Timing differences are normal.

**What I do watch for:** if the vault custodian's closing inventory grows significantly month on month, that could signal a problem — maybe the pouring schedule is disrupted, or there's a backlog. But that's an operational question, not an accrual question.

Now walk me through the calculation itself. You've got the three inputs in hand — the Met team's monthly production data, the gold price from group, and the vault custodian's poured ounces figure. What do you actually do with them? Take me through the steps of building the accrual."

Right. Here's how the calculation works.

**Step one: Calculate the ounces of gold in the doré that was poured.**

The Met team gives me: tonnes milled, head grade, recovery percentage. From that, I calculate total gold ounces produced using the standard formula:

Gold ounces produced = Tonnes milled × Head grade (g/tonne) × Recovery % ÷ 31.1035

That gives me the theoretical gold content. But doré isn't pure gold — it contains gold and silver and some other impurities depending on the circuit design. Our doré is typically around 70–75% gold by weight, 20–25% silver. So I then apply the doré assay — I get a monthly assay from the refinery that tells me the actual gold percentage in the doré we produced that month.

So: Ounces of gold in total doré produced = [Tonnes milled × Head grade ÷ 31.1035 × Recovery %] × Gold assay %

But I don't recognise all of that as revenue in the current month. I only recognise the poured portion. So:

**Ounces of gold in poured doré = [Gold ounces produced] × [Poured doré weight ÷ Total doré weight produced]**

The vault custodian tells me the poured weight in ounces of doré. The Met team tells me the total doré weight produced. I calculate what percentage of total doré was actually poured, and I apply that percentage to the gold ounces I calculated above.

**Step two: Apply the gold price.**

I take the poured gold ounces and multiply by the gold price I've been given by group.

Revenue accrual = Poured gold ounces × Gold price per ounce

That's the number. That's what goes into the journal entry.

**Step three: Build the journal entry.**

Dr. Accounts receivable (or prepayment, depending on whether we've invoiced yet) — the accrual amount
Cr. Revenue — the accrual amount

I typically also add a description line that shows the calculation — "Revenue accrual: [X] ounces doré × [Y]% gold assay × ZAR [price] per ounce."

Then I send that journal entry to Sarah in central finance for posting. I send it in the same format as the other BPs — a spreadsheet with account codes, amounts, and description.

**Now — there are some nuances I should flag:**

One: The head grade and recovery figures from the Met team are measured. But sometimes there's a lag between measurement and final assay, or the monthly weighted average gets slightly adjusted as final assays come back. I usually have the data by the third or fourth of the month, but occasionally I'm working with preliminary figures and I'll have to reverse and re-accrue if the final assay comes back materially different. That's rare, but it happens maybe once or twice a year.

Two: The gold price. Group sometimes changes the price assumption without telling me. I've had the revenue policy updated and not been notified, which means I'll accrue using one price and then later find out I should have used a different one. When I discover that, I have to reverse the prior month's accrual and re-accrue at the correct price. It's a reconciliation nightmare and I've complained about it twice, but it keeps happening.

Three: The doré assay — I get that from the refinery, but it's not always available on the date I need it. If the refinery assay is delayed, I use a trailing three-month average assay as a placeholder. Once the actual assay comes back, I reverse and re-accrue.

So the calculation itself is straightforward. But there are three moving pieces — assay, gold price, and poured weight — any of which can require a reversal and re-accrual if it changes after I've posted.

Entry 1 (reversal):
Dr. Revenue — the original accrual amount
Cr. Accounts receivable — the original accrual amount

Entry 2 (correcting entry):
Dr. Accounts receivable — the correct accrual amount
Cr. Revenue — the correct accrual amount

Both entries go in the same email, with a description that says something like: "Reversal of provisional revenue accrual posted [date] — gold price updated by group from $2,000 to $2,020 per ounce. Corrected entry reflects actual price."

Right. Let me walk through it by cost category.

**Reagent accruals:**

Step one: Pull the consumption data. I extract the daily dispensing logs from the plant's reagent tracking system — we have about eight primary reagents across the leaching, elution, and tailings circuits. The system logs quantity dispensed each day. I pull the month's complete data and total it by reagent by circuit.

Step two: Apply the unit cost. I look up each reagent in my standing data file — that sheet has the current contracted price per unit. So if we dispensed 1,200 litres of leaching acid in June and the contract rate is ZAR 45 per litre, the accrual is 1,200 × 45 = ZAR 54,000.

Step three: Map to the correct cost centre. Using my cost centre mapping sheet, I determine whether this reagent charge is 4210 (general reagent) or whether it needs to be split — for example, if the mapping says "leaching acid codes to 4210 sub-centre A," I'll code it accordingly.

Step four: Build the journal entry:

Dr. Cost of goods sold / Plant operating costs [appropriate account] — the accrual amount
Cr. Accrued expenses / Accounts payable — the accrual amount

I do this for each reagent separately, so if I have eight reagents, that's potentially eight lines in the journal, each coded to the appropriate cost centre and account.

**Energy accruals:**

This is more complex because the invoice is almost never available at month-end.

Step one: Pull the metered consumption. We have a power monitoring system that logs consumption hourly. I extract the month's total consumption in kWh.

Step two: Calculate the accrual using the contracted rate. We have a tiered energy contract — different rates depending on time of use and peak demand. I have a spreadsheet in my standing data file that breaks down the rates: base rate, peak surcharge, transmission charges, et cetera. I apply those rates to the metered consumption.

So if we consumed 300,000 kWh in June, and our blended average rate is ZAR 1.85 per kWh, the accrual is 300,000 × 1.85 = ZAR 555,000.

Step three: Build the journal entry:

Dr. Cost of goods sold / Energy — the accrual amount
Cr. Accrued expenses — the accrual amount

**Now — the complication with energy:** The actual invoice usually arrives mid-month. When it does, I reverse the accrual and post the actual invoice amount. So every month I'm doing:

Accrual posted on day 4: ZAR 555,000
Invoice arrives on day 14 or 16: actual amount is ZAR 542,000
I reverse: Dr. Accrued expenses 555,000 / Cr. Energy cost 555,000
I post the actual: Dr. Energy cost 542,000 / Cr. Accounts payable 542,000

Net result: energy cost of ZAR 542,000 in the month. But I've created two reversing entries to get there.

I've raised this with central finance and suggested we could move to an accrual methodology that's more predictable — accruing based on a 12-month average rate, settling the variance quarterly when the invoice arrives. That would eliminate the monthly reversals. But again, that's a policy decision and it hasn't been implemented.

**Other cost accruals:**

Beyond reagents and energy, there are occasional month-end timing accruals — contractors who've worked but haven't invoiced, spare parts that have been consumed but not yet charged, that sort of thing. These are lower volume and less systematic. I review the plant cost centres in Pronto at month-end and if I see an obvious gap — for example, maintenance work that was completed but not invoiced — I'll accrue it. But these aren't formulaic.

**Step four — compile and send:**

I compile all the cost accruals into a single spreadsheet with columns for: date, account code, cost centre, description, amount. I send this to Sarah in the same format as the revenue accrual. Usually by day 5.

**Exception scenarios:**

One: A reagent contract price changes mid-month. If the new rate takes effect on day 15, I have to split the accrual — first half of the month at the old rate, second half at the new rate. I have to track this manually because the dispensing system doesn't know about rate changes.

Two: Energy consumption is abnormally high or low. If we had an unplanned shutdown or a processing campaign that was unusually intensive, the consumption spikes. I don't second-guess it — I accrual based on the actual metered consumption. But I'll flag it in the journal description if it's materially different from the prior month.

Three: Reagent consumption data is incomplete. If the dispensing system didn't log a day's data (system downtime, calibration), I estimate it based on the surrounding days' average. That estimate gets flagged in the accrual description.

Four: A contract is renegotiated and I haven't updated the standing data file yet. This is rare but it's happened — someone negotiates a new rate with a vendor and doesn't tell me immediately. I find out when the invoice arrives and it doesn't match my accrual. Then I reverse and re-accrue at the correct rate.

**Quality check before posting:**

Before I send the accruals to Sarah, I do a sanity check. I compare this month's total accrual to the prior month and to the budget. If June's reagent accrual is 30% higher than May and I know there was no operational change, I'll drill into the data to see why. Usually it's a data entry error or a contract rate update I missed. **The first thing I do:** I build a standalone Excel model. Not in Pronto, not in a template — just a clean model that's mine to work in. That model has three sections:

Section one is the production assumptions. Tonnes milled by quarter, average head grade by quarter, recovery percentage by quarter. These come from the Plant Manager. I'll also ask him about any planned shutdowns, campaigns, or operational changes that might affect the budget — for example, "are we planning to change the leach residence time, which would affect reagent consumption?"

Section two is the consumption model. For each cost category — reagents, energy, labour, maintenance, et cetera — I have a consumption rate per tonne of ore processed. These rates come from my standing data file, but I review them annually. So if leaching acid consumption has been running at 15 kg per tonne based on historical average, but the Plant Manager says we're going to optimize the dosing and bring it down to 14 kg per tonne, I update that assumption.

Then I multiply the annual tonnes by the per-tonne consumption rates to get total consumption for the year.

Section three is the cost build. For each cost category, I take the total consumption and multiply by the unit cost — again from the standing data file, updated for any known contract changes or price escalations.

So the flow is: Tonnes × Head grade × Recovery → Gold production → Operating cost per tonne → Total operating cost.

**And then I also add fixed costs** — labour, depreciation, allocations from corporate — which don't vary with tonnes but are part of the budget.

That's the model. Once it's built and I'm satisfied with the assumptions, I extract the cost centre totals and send them to the Budget Analyst for loading into Pronto.
